root_motivation: The monthly draft ritual produces 2-4 letter Markdown files per month in ~/.claude/letters-we-never-sent/<year>/. Phase 1 is the curation tool jsy needs to triage drafts: read, accept/decline/edit, optionally mark `send-for-real`. Without it, drafts accumulate in a directory and the annual binding ritual has no signal for which letters made the cut.
user_persona: jsy at the end of each month (or whenever the practice calls), running the curation CLI over ~/.claude/letters-we-never-sent/<year>/ to triage drafts before the annual binding ritual. Audience: jsy himself, alone, in a focused reading session.

unfakeable_metric.name: acceptance_tests_passing
unfakeable_metric.harness_command: cargo test --tests 2>&1 | grep -E 'test result: ok' | wc -l

acceptance_criteria.description: `letter list [--root <dir>] [--year <YYYY>] [--state <all|pending|accepted|declined|send-real>]` prints a one-line-per-letter table to stdout: `<filename> <state> <accepted_at|->  <subject-line>`. Default --root = `~/.claude/letters-we-never-sent`. Default --state = `pending`.

Each letter is a Markdown file with YAML frontmatter. Recognised frontmatter keys: `recipient` (anonymized, e.g. `the PM`), `month` (YYYY-MM), `state` (one of `pending|accepted|declined|send-real`, default `pending`), `accepted_at` (RFC3339, set when state moves to non-pending), `subject` (the first H1 or filename if absent).

`letter accept <filename> [--root <dir>]` rewrites the file's frontmatter to set state=accepted and accepted_at=now(RFC3339). The body content is preserved byte-for-byte after the frontmatter. Exit 0 on success.

`letter decline <filename> [--root <dir>]` sets state=declined and accepted_at=now. Same byte-for-byte body preservation. `letter mark-send-real <filename>` sets state=send-real. All three state transitions are idempotent (re-running the same command on the same file is a no-op other than updating accepted_at).

Letters without frontmatter, or with malformed YAML, are surfaced in `list` output as state=`(unparseable)` and skipped from state changes (`accept`/`decline` exits 3 with stderr explaining why). The CLI never crashes on malformed input.

Filenames are positional arguments; both relative-to-root (`NN.md`) and absolute paths are accepted. Glob patterns (e.g. `2026-05-*.md`) are NOT auto-expanded — the user uses shell globbing for that. Single filename per invocation.
`letter stats [--root <dir>] [--year <YYYY>]` prints aggregate counts to stdout: `accepted: N, declined: N, pending: N, send-real: N, total: N`. Multi-line, machine-grep-able.
`letter open <filename> [--root <dir>]` invokes $EDITOR on the file (so the user can edit in-place during a curation session). Exit 0 if editor exits 0.

scope: Read/write Markdown files with YAML frontmatter under a root directory
List letters by year and state
Preserve body bytes exactly across frontmatter updates
non_goals: Draft generation (LLM-call producing letter drafts) — that's the monthly Claude prompt, separate workflow
Encryption at rest (the user uses gocryptfs separately if desired)
Sending actual letters (send-real marks intent; sending is manual)

five_whys_trace.q: Why a Phase 1 curation CLI when Phase 0 of the PRD says `monthly Claude prompt; drafts saved as Markdown`?
five_whys_trace.a: Because Phase 0 produces files and Phase 2 typesets them — without Phase 1's accept/decline, the typesetter has no signal for which letters made the cut. The state field on each letter IS the curation result.

five_whys_trace.q: Why store state in the frontmatter rather than in a sidecar SQLite index?
five_whys_trace.a: Because the letters ARE the artifact — they should be self-describing. A sidecar makes the state easy to lose; frontmatter travels with the file through any future migration (encryption, backup, hand-edit).

Because the user may hand-edit the body in $EDITOR between state transitions — and any rewriting by the curation tool would risk losing whitespace, em-dashes, or other characters the user cared about. The body is sacred.
